chunk_summary: Early referral is critical for children with secondary or genetic metabolic phenotypes, including lipodystrophy, hypercortisolism, and syndromic obesity, to ensure tailored interventions and avoid ineffective lifestyle counseling.
learning_objectives: Identify phenotypes requiring early endocrine or genetic referral in children with metabolic risk.
Understand clinical signs of lipodystrophy, hypercortisolism, and syndromic obesity.
Learn referral thresholds for genetic counseling and specialty services in pediatric obesity.
Document phenotypes explicitly to prioritize referral urgency and avoid repetitive advice.
key_facts: Severe insulin resistance disproportionate to adiposity warrants early endocrine referral.
Lipodystrophy may present with marked acanthosis, low subcutaneous fat, very high triglycerides, fatty liver, or early-onset diabetes.
Hypercortisolism is suspected with weight gain, growth deceleration, proximal muscle weakness, violaceous striae, and hypertension.
Syndromic or monogenic obesity often has early onset (<5 years), hyperphagia, dysmorphic features, developmental delay, or autosomal inheritance patterns.
Consanguinity lowers the threshold for genetic referral in India.
reference_quote: Suspect lipodystrophy when there is marked acanthosis with relatively low subcutaneous fat, very high TG, fatty liver, or early-onset diabetes. This phenotype can present as apparent 'lean diabetes' with extreme dyslipidemia and requires specialist evaluation for genetic or acquired lipodystrophy and tailored therapy. (Source: IAP guidelines on childhood obesity)
document_key_topics: Referral thresholds for endocrine and genetic evaluation
Tailored interventions for pediatric metabolic disorders
